Great news — I can see from your conversation history that you've recently gotten into slow cooking and have been exploring a variety of recipes, including beef stew and slow cooker yogurt! Based on your history and general slow cooker expertise, here are some tailored tips to help you get better results:

---

### 🥩 For Meat-Based Dishes (like your Beef Stew)
- **Don't skip browning** — Searing meat before adding it to the slow cooker adds a depth of flavour you can't get otherwise.
- **Use the right cuts** — Tougher, fattier cuts (like chuck roast or pork shoulder) work best. They break down beautifully over long cooking times.
- **Don't overcook lean meats** — Chicken breasts, for example, can dry out. Add them later in the cooking process or switch to thighs.

---

### 🌡️ Temperature & Timing
- **Low and slow is usually better** — Cooking on LOW for 6–8 hours generally yields more tender, flavourful results than HIGH for 3–4 hours.
- **Resist lifting the lid** — Every peek adds 15–20 minutes to your cook time by releasing heat and steam.
- **Fill it correctly** — Aim for the slow cooker to be ½ to ¾ full for best results.

---

### 🧄 Flavour Tips
- **Layer your seasonings** — Add hardy spices (like cumin or chili powder) at the start, but save fresh herbs and delicate seasonings for the last 30 minutes.
- **Reduce liquid** — Slow cookers trap moisture, so use less liquid than a stovetop recipe calls for. You can always thicken the sauce at the end.
- **Finish with acid** — A splash of lemon juice or vinegar stirred in at the end brightens the whole dish.

---

### 🥛 For Your Slow Cooker Yogurt
Since you've been experimenting with yogurt (including cashew-based versions), a key tip is:
- **Temperature control is everything** — Make sure the milk heats to 180°F before cooling to 110–120°F before adding your starter culture. Too hot will kill the cultures; too cool and they won't activate.
- **Don't rush incubation** — Give it the full 8–12 hours for the best thickness and tang.

---

I don't have any specific past conversations about phone battery life issues, but based on your history I can see you're someone who uses tech accessories like portable power banks — so you're clearly mindful about staying charged! Here are some practical tips to help improve your phone's battery life:

---

### 🔋 Tips to Improve Phone Battery Life

**Reduce Screen Usage**
- Lower your screen brightness or enable **auto-brightness**.
- Shorten your **screen timeout** so it turns off sooner when idle.

**Manage Background Activity**
- Turn off **background app refresh** for apps that don't need it.
- Disable **location services** for apps that don't require it constantly.
- Turn off **push notifications** for non-essential apps.

**Connectivity Settings**
- Turn off **Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, or mobile data** when not in use.
- Disable **5G** if you don't need it — it drains battery faster than 4G.
- Turn on **Airplane Mode** in low-signal areas (your phone works harder to find signal).

**Battery & Charging Habits**
- Avoid letting your battery drop to 0% regularly — try to keep it between **20–80%** for long-term battery health.
- Avoid charging overnight or leaving it plugged in at 100% for extended periods.
- Use **Low Power Mode** (iOS) or **Battery Saver** (Android) when running low.

**App & Software Maintenance**
- Check your **battery usage stats** in settings to identify which apps are draining the most.
- Keep your phone's **software updated**, as updates often include battery optimisations.
- Uninstall or restrict battery-hungry apps.

**On-the-Go Solution**
- Since you've previously looked into **portable power banks**, keeping one handy (like the Anker PowerCore or Jackery Titan S you explored before) is a great backup for when you're out and about!

### 🍪 Tips to Take Your Chocolate Chip Cookies to the Next Level

**1. Upgrade Your Sugar**
Since you've shown interest in specialty sugars, try swapping some of the white sugar for **brown sugar, muscovado, or demerara**. These add deeper caramel and molasses notes that make cookies taste richer and more complex.

**2. Brown Your Butter**
This is one of the biggest game-changers. Browning the butter before mixing adds a nutty, toffee-like depth that regular melted or softened butter just can't match.

**3. Add a Pinch of Flaky Sea Salt**
A sprinkle of flaky sea salt on top before baking enhances sweetness and creates a beautiful contrast — a classic trick that makes a huge difference.

**4. Rest the Dough**
Chill your dough for at least 24–72 hours in the fridge. This allows the flavors to develop and results in a chewier, more complex cookie.

**5. Experiment with Mix-Ins**
- A splash of **espresso powder** intensifies the chocolate flavor without making it taste like coffee.
- Try **toasted nuts** (walnuts or pecans) for crunch and depth.
- Mix in a bit of **dark chocolate** alongside the chips for a more sophisticated flavor.

**6. Use High-Quality Chocolate**
Chop a good-quality chocolate bar instead of (or in addition to) chips — the irregular pieces melt differently and create those gorgeous chocolate puddles.

**7. Tweak Your Flour**
Since you've been experimenting with different flours, try using **bread flour** instead of all-purpose for a chewier texture, or add a tablespoon of **cornstarch** for a softer bite.

I can see from your history that you've been interested in **mid-century modern design** for your bedroom — specifically working on a new dresser with walnut wood! That's great context. Here are some tips for rearranging your bedroom furniture, with that style in mind:

---

### 🛏️ General Bedroom Furniture Arrangement Tips

1. **Start with the bed as the focal point.** The bed is the largest piece, so position it first — ideally against the main wall, centered if possible, and away from windows and doors for better sleep quality.

2. **Measure before you move.** Sketch out your room dimensions and furniture sizes beforehand to avoid heavy lifting for nothing. Free tools like **Roomstyler** or **IKEA's room planner** can help.

3. **Ensure good traffic flow.** Leave at least **24–36 inches** of walkway around the bed and other major pieces so the room feels open and easy to navigate.

4. **Balance visual weight.** Distribute heavier furniture (like your dresser or wardrobe) evenly around the room so it doesn't feel lopsided. Mid-century modern pieces with tapered legs help the room feel lighter and more airy.

5. **Consider natural light.** Avoid placing your dresser mirror directly opposite a window — it can create glare. Instead, angle it to reflect light into darker corners.

6. **Keep the dresser accessible.** Place it somewhere with enough clearance to open all drawers fully — typically at least **3 feet** in front.

7. **Think about symmetry.** Nightstands flanking the bed create a balanced, polished look that pairs well with the mid-century modern aesthetic you've been going for.

8. **Declutter first.** Before rearranging, it's a great opportunity to clear out items you no longer need — a less cluttered room makes any layout feel better.

---

Just a few days ago (May 25th), you were exploring the differences between a **Fender Stratocaster** and a **Gibson Les Paul**, and diving into topics like fingerpicking, open D tuning, and tone. Here are some personalised tips for your music store visit based on what you've been looking into:

---

### 🎸 Tips for Your Music Store Visit

**1. Revisit the Strat vs. Les Paul debate — in person!**
You've been comparing these two icons. Now's your chance to actually *play* both and feel the difference:
- The **Stratocaster** is great for clean, crisp tones and versatility across genres.
- The **Les Paul** shines for rock, blues, and heavier styles with its warm, full tone and sustain.

**2. Think about your playing style**
Based on your recent questions, you've shown interest in:
- **Fingerpicking** (e.g., "Blackbird"-style playing) — if that's a priority, consider a guitar with a narrower neck and lower action, or even an acoustic/semi-hollow.
- **Open D tuning** — if you want to explore slide or roots music, make sure the guitar can be set up comfortably for alternate tunings.

**3. Check the neck feel**
Play both a Strat-style (bolt-on, 25.5" scale) and a Les Paul-style (set-in neck, 24.75" scale). The shorter scale of the Les Paul can feel easier for bending, while the Strat's longer scale gives a snappier feel.

**4. Test the pickups**
- Single-coils (Strat) = bright, clear, with some hum
- Humbuckers (Les Paul) = warm, thick, noise-free
Ask the store to plug you into a clean amp so you can hear the guitar's natural character.

**5. Don't forget playability**
- Check the **action** (string height) — lower is easier to play, but too low causes buzzing.
- Make sure the **intonation** is good (notes should stay in tune up the neck).
- Consider **string gauge** — lighter strings are easier to play, heavier strings give more tone.

**6. Try before you buy**
Sit down, play some of your favourite riffs or patterns, and see which guitar feels like "home" — as you noted in your research, that's ultimately the most important factor!

---

---

🚇 **Getting Around Tokyo — Tips Just for You**

### 1. **Get a Suica Card First**
This is the single most important thing! Pick one up as soon as you arrive at the airport. It's a prepaid IC card you can tap in and out with at train and bus stations across Tokyo — no need to buy individual tickets each time. Keep an eye on it throughout your trip.

### 2. **From Narita Airport → Shinjuku (Your Hotel Area)**
You have two main options:
- **JR Narita Express (N'EX):** ~60–70 mins, ~¥3,200–3,500. More comfortable and direct.
- **Keisei Skyliner + JR Yamanote Line:** ~50–60 mins, ~¥2,500–3,000. Slightly cheaper.
- **Keisei Limited Express + JR Yamanote Line:** ~70–80 mins, ~¥2,000–2,500. Most budget-friendly.

### 3. **Use Google Maps or a Transit App**
Plan your routes in advance. Google Maps is excellent for Tokyo's train network and will show you transfers, platform numbers, and journey times.

### 4. **Getting to Key Spots (e.g. Tsukiji Fish Market)**
From Shinjuku: Take the **JR Yamanote Line** to Shimbashi, then transfer to the **Tokyo Metro Ginza Line** to Tsukijishijo Station (~30–40 mins, ~¥420–450).

### 5. **Day Trips (e.g. Nikko National Park)**
Your Suica card works on the **JR Tohoku Shinkansen** to Utsunomiya, then the **JR Nikko Line** to Nikko Station. Plan your route in advance and check schedules.

### 6. **Don't Hesitate to Ask for Help**
Station staff in Tokyo are generally very helpful, even with language barriers. There are also English signs at most major stations.

### 7. **Money Tips**
- Use **7-Eleven ATMs** or ATMs at Shinjuku Station to withdraw yen — they accept international cards and offer good rates.
- Inform your bank of your travel plans before you go to avoid blocked transactions.

---

Sure! Here's a recap of the refining processes used at CITGO's **Lake Charles Refinery** in Louisiana:

3. **Alkylation** — Combines smaller molecules to form larger ones, creating high-octane gasoline components.

4. **Hydrotreating** — Removes impurities from the gasoline and diesel fractions to improve their overall quality.

From our previous conversation, the **6S (Second Simulation of a Satellite Signal in the Solar Spectrum)** algorithm is the one implemented in the **SIAC_GEE** tool.

To recap the distinction between the three algorithms mentioned:
- **6S** → implemented in **SIAC_GEE**
- **MAJA** → implemented in the **MAJA software** developed by the French Space Agency (CNES)
- **Sen2Cor** → implemented in the **Sen2Cor software** developed by ESA

The recommended ratio is **1:10** — that is, **1 part tea tree oil to 10 parts carrier oil**.

So for every few drops of tea tree oil, you'd mix in 10 times that amount of a carrier oil such as **coconut oil, jojoba oil, or almond oil**. This dilution is important because applying tea tree oil directly to the skin can cause irritation, redness, or allergic reactions.

Also, it's always a good idea to do a **patch test** first — apply a small amount of the diluted mixture to your forearm and wait to see if any redness or itching occurs before using it more broadly.
